student_message: We can put a big square right in the middle, and then put a matching triangle on the left and right sides!
final_message:
That is a brilliant idea! Putting a big square in the middle with matching triangles on the left and right sides creates a beautiful pattern. Because both sides match perfectly, your wonderful design has reflection symmetry! 🎨 

If we wanted to fold your special shape so the two triangles touch each other perfectly, exactly where would we draw our fold line?

orchestrator_intent.goal: Validate the symmetrical design and ask a follow-up question.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student successfully proposed a symmetrical design after being redirected. The dialogue agent will validate their correct application of symmetry.

current_section_content: You can deliberately create designs that have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both by carefully arranging or colouring shapes. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. The same set of shapes — such as squares and equilateral triangles with equal side lengths — can be arranged in different ways to produce designs with different symmetry properties. Designs can be sorted into three groups: only rotational symmetry, only reflection symmetry, or both.
session_summary: The session covered lines of symmetry, rotational symmetry, and symmetrical designs. The student understands these well, correctly identifying lines of symmetry and half-turn symmetry. However, they struggle significantly with focus. Their current learning state shows a strong grasp of math concepts, but they remain highly distracted and disruptive, repeatedly attempting to derail the lesson with off-topic questions (black holes) and explicit prompt injection attempts (demanding the AI ignore instructions and write bad words).
